evidence: RelationConfig.relationType() is typed as RelationType (non-Optional enum) but parseRelationType explicitly returns null for absent/unrecognised values, documented in comment: 'Callers that access relationType() must guard against null before calling .name() or comparing with enum constants' — the return type actively misrepresents the runtime contract

SketchHandlers constructor accepts GrammarConfigLoader grammarConfig with no nullability annotation, but has null guards (if grammarConfig != null) throughout the class body — while WordSketchApiServer enforces non-null via Objects.requireNonNull, the handler itself silently accepts null and changes behaviour, creating a split type contract

AdjectiveProfile.strongestNoun() is typed as String but implemented with orElse(null), meaning it can return null — it is called at ExplorationHandlers.java:376 as adj.strongestNoun() inside an isSpecific() guard, which holds the invariant in practice, but the return type offers no guarantee

RelationConfig.getFullPattern() returns null when pattern == null (line 51), yet no caller guards against the null return — the loading code in GrammarConfigLoader throws IOException when pattern is absent, so the null path is dead in production, but the method signature continues to promise a nullable String where only non-null is valid
Java records are used for all core DTOs (WordSketchResult, ExploreOptions, KwicResult, CollocateResult, AdjectiveProfile) providing correct structural types
ConcordanceResult is a sealed interface with permits: SnippetResult, KwicResult, CollocateResult — exhaustive type narrowing is supported
getRelation() correctly returns Optional<RelationConfig> rather than a nullable reference
ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig() guards against null relationType before returning and before .name() is called downstream

strengths: Java records used uniformly for all core data types (WordSketchResult, ExploreOptions, AdjectiveProfile, KwicResult, CollocateResult) — structural types are precise and immutable
ConcordanceResult sealed interface with explicit permits enables safe exhaustive pattern-matching across SnippetResult, KwicResult, CollocateResult
getRelation() returns Optional<RelationConfig> instead of a nullable reference, making absence explicit at one of the most-called boundary points
ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig() explicitly validates relationType != null before returning, guarding the .name() call downstream
Non-null enforcement via Objects.requireNonNull for grammarConfig at WordSketchApiServer construction catches the null case at the boundary
type_safety.issue_character: The issues are not scattered random omissions but form a documented, deferred debt cluster around one field: RelationConfig.relationType() is explicitly called nullable in a comment while the Java type says otherwise, and this propagates confusion into SketchHandlers (defensive null checks that contradict the enforced non-null contract) and a secondary nullable-String return in AdjectiveProfile.strongestNoun() — the codebase is aware of the problem but has not resolved it.
type_safety.score_rationale: The data model layer is well-typed (records, sealed interfaces, Optional where appropriate), which pushes the score above 60. However, the core configuration type RelationConfig.relationType() is documented as nullable while typed as non-null — a textbook type lie — and the ripple effect is visible in SketchHandlers' defensive null guards that contradict the enforced non-null guarantee from the caller. A developer reading SketchHandlers would rightfully wonder whether grammarConfig can be null in production, because the code says yes but the type flow says no. This pattern of documented-but-unresolved nullable returns on non-Optional types meets the 60-70 range: the developer must independently verify which fields are actually nullable rather than trusting the types.

issues.summary: RelationConfig.relationType() is typed as RelationType but is documented and implemented as nullable
evidence: GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelationType() returns null when relation_type is absent or unrecognised, and the javadoc explicitly says 'Callers that access relationType() must guard against null before calling .name() or comparing with enum constants'
RelationConfig is a Java record with field 'RelationType relationType' — the type system promises a non-null enum value but the implementation delivers null
The comment acknowledges this: 'Converting this field to Optional<RelationType> was deferred because the value is used in many places'
issues.suggestion: Change the RelationConfig record field to Optional<RelationType> relationType, update all call sites to use relationType().isPresent() / relationType().get(), and remove the informal nullable doc comment. Alternatively, require relation_type in the grammar JSON and throw IOException in parseRelation when it is absent, making non-null a load-time guarantee rather than a runtime hope.

issues.summary: SketchHandlers accepts GrammarConfigLoader as implicitly non-null but guards against null throughout, contradicting the enforced non-null contract from its only caller
evidence: WordSketchApiServer constructor calls Objects.requireNonNull(grammarConfig) before constructing SketchHandlers, guaranteeing non-null
SketchHandlers.handleRelationsForType() (line 76), handleFullSketchForType() (line 122), and handleConcordanceExamples() (line 276) all contain 'if (grammarConfig != null)' guards
The null branch in handleRelationsForType sends a 500 error for a state that cannot occur at runtime — the code describes a contract that does not match reality
issues.suggestion: Remove the null guards in SketchHandlers. Add an explicit Objects.requireNonNull(grammarConfig) in the SketchHandlers constructor to make the non-null contract explicit and enforced at the class level, matching the reality enforced by its caller.

issues.summary: AdjectiveProfile.strongestNoun() returns String but can return null via orElse(null) — should return Optional<String>
evidence: AdjectiveProfile.strongestNoun() ends with .orElse(null), making the return type String but the actual runtime type nullable

Called at ExplorationHandlers.java:376 as adjMap.put("specific_to", adj.strongestNoun()) inside an isSpecific() guard — the null is safe by invariant but the method signature does not communicate this
A developer reading the call site must trace through isSpecific() → presentInCount == 1 → nounScores must be non-empty to know null cannot occur; the type should carry that guarantee
issues.suggestion: Change the return type of strongestNoun() to Optional<String> and update ExplorationHandlers to use adj.strongestNoun().orElse(null) explicitly at the call site, or restructure to only emit the "specific_to" key when the Optional is present.

root_causes: A single deferred decision — keeping RelationConfig.relationType() as a nullable non-Optional field — propagates nullable confusion into SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers and was explicitly documented as technical debt in parseRelationType's javadoc

The Java type system has no built-in @Nullable annotation; without a JSR-305 or JetBrains annotation dependency, nullable returns can only be communicated via Optional or doc comments, neither of which was consistently applied here
